content.text: The Azure Pricing Calculator is a web-based tool that lets you build and share cost estimates for any combination of Azure services before you deploy anything. You add services, configure their parameters (region, SKU, hours per month, tier), and the calculator computes a monthly estimate. Estimates can be saved, exported to Excel, and shared via URL.

For automated and programmatic pricing queries — such as fetching live prices in a CI/CD pipeline or building internal cost dashboards — the Azure Retail Prices API provides the same data without authentication, accessible from any HTTP client.

attrs.alt: Azure Pricing Calculator workflow and Retail Prices API overview

content.text: The calculator at https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/calculator/ organises estimates into named scenarios. Each scenario can contain multiple services and reflects a snapshot of current pricing.
Navigate to the Pricing Calculator and click Add to estimate.
Search for a service (e.g. Virtual Machines) and click Add.
Configure the service: choose region, OS, instance type, hours/month, and payment option (PAYG, 1-year RI, 3-year RI).
Toggle Azure Hybrid Benefit if applicable.
Repeat for each service in your architecture.
Review the total at the bottom. Click Export to download an Excel file or Save to get a shareable URL.
Saved estimates are public links — anyone with the URL can view the configuration. Do not include confidential architecture details or internal naming conventions in the estimate description.

The Retail Prices API exposes the same pricing data used by the calculator. It requires no authentication and supports OData $filter queries. The API is the right tool when you need pricing data programmatically: cost estimation scripts, IaC cost gates, Terraform cost checks.

# Query current PAYG price for Standard_D4s_v5 Linux in East US
curl -s "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices?api-version=2023-01-01-preview&\$filter=serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' and skuName eq 'D4s v5' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' and priceType eq 'Consumption' and operatingSystemFamily eq 'Linux'" \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"retailPrice"|"skuName"|"meterName"'

# Query 1-year reservation price for the same SKU
curl -s "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices?api-version=2023-01-01-preview&\$filter=serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' and skuName eq 'D4s v5' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' and priceType eq 'Reservation'" \
  | python3 -m json.tool

A cost estimation script queries the Retail Prices API for each service in your architecture and produces a monthly total. This is useful for validating that a new architecture design falls within budget before deploying.

import requests
from dataclasses import dataclass

BASE_URL = "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices"
API_VERSION = "2023-01-01-preview"

@dataclass
class ServiceEstimate:
    name: str
    sku: str
    region: str
    hours_per_month: float
    quantity: int = 1
    service_name: str = "Virtual Machines"
    os_family: str = "Linux"

def fetch_payg_price(estimate: ServiceEstimate) -> float:
    params = {
        "api-version": API_VERSION,
        "$filter": (
            f"serviceName eq '{estimate.service_name}' "
            f"and skuName eq '{estimate.sku}' "
            f"and armRegionName eq '{estimate.region}' "
            f"and priceType eq 'Consumption' "
            f"and contains(productName, '{estimate.os_family}') "
            f"and not contains(skuName, 'Spot')"
        ),
    }
    resp = requests.get(BASE_URL, params=params, timeout=10)
    items = resp.json().get("Items", [])
    if not items:
        print(f"  WARNING: No price found for {estimate.sku} in {estimate.region}")
        return 0.0
    return items[0].get("retailPrice", 0.0)

# Define the architecture
architecture = [
    ServiceEstimate("Web tier VMs", "D4s v5", "eastus", 730, quantity=3),
    ServiceEstimate("App tier VMs", "D8s v5", "eastus", 730, quantity=2),
    ServiceEstimate("Batch workers", "D4s v5", "eastus", 200, quantity=5),
]

print(f"{'Service':<25} {'SKU':<12} {'Qty':>4} {'Hrs':>6} {'$/hr':>8} {'Monthly':>10}")
print("-" * 70)
total_monthly = 0.0
for svc in architecture:
    price_hr = fetch_payg_price(svc)
    monthly = price_hr * svc.hours_per_month * svc.quantity
    total_monthly += monthly
    print(f"{svc.name:<25} {svc.sku:<12} {svc.quantity:>4} {svc.hours_per_month:>6.0f} ${price_hr:>7.4f} ${monthly:>9.2f}")

print("-" * 70)
print(f"{'Total monthly estimate':>55} ${total_monthly:>9.2f}")
print(f"{'Annual estimate':>55} ${total_monthly * 12:>9.2f}")
Prices for the same VM SKU vary by region. Running a batch job in a cheaper region can reduce costs significantly. The Retail Prices API makes it easy to query and compare across regions.

import requests

def compare_regions(sku: str, regions: list) -> list:
    """Return sorted list of (region, price) for a given VM SKU."""
    results = []
    for region in regions:
        params = {
            "api-version": "2023-01-01-preview",
            "$filter": (
                f"serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' "
                f"and skuName eq '{sku}' "
                f"and armRegionName eq '{region}' "
                f"and priceType eq 'Consumption' "
                f"and contains(productName, 'Linux') "
                f"and not contains(skuName, 'Spot')"
            ),
        }
        resp = requests.get("https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices", params=params, timeout=10)
        items = resp.json().get("Items", [])
        if items:
            results.append((region, items[0].get("retailPrice", 0)))
    return sorted(results, key=lambda x: x[1])

regions = ["eastus", "westus2", "northeurope", "eastasia", "australiaeast", "brazilsouth"]
rankings = compare_regions("D4s v5", regions)

print(f"D4s v5 Linux PAYG prices by region:")
cheapest_price = rankings[0][1] if rankings else 1
for region, price in rankings:
    pct_vs_cheapest = ((price - cheapest_price) / cheapest_price * 100) if cheapest_price > 0 else 0
    bar = "+" * int(pct_vs_cheapest / 2)
    print(f"  {region:<20} ${price:.4f}/hr  {bar} (+{pct_vs_cheapest:.1f}% vs cheapest)")
Azure provides two estimation tools with different purposes. Understanding which to use avoids confusion when presenting cost justifications to stakeholders.
Current on-premises infrastructure (servers, storage, networking, labour)
Business case for cloud migration, CFO presentations

The TCO Calculator factors in hidden on-premises costs: hardware refresh cycles, data centre space, power, cooling, IT labour, and licensing. For a migration business case, the TCO Calculator output is usually more persuasive to finance stakeholders than a raw Azure bill estimate.
Using the Pricing API in CI/CD Cost Gates

A cost gate is a CI/CD step that queries the Retail Prices API for the services about to be deployed and blocks the pipeline if estimated cost exceeds a threshold. This prevents expensive misconfigurations from reaching production.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""cost_gate.py — fail the CI pipeline if estimated monthly cost exceeds budget.
Usage: python3 cost_gate.py --budget 500 --config cost-config.json
"""
import argparse, json, sys, requests

def fetch_price(service: str, sku: str, region: str, price_type: str = "Consumption") -> float:
    params = {
        "api-version": "2023-01-01-preview",
        "$filter": (
            f"serviceName eq '{service}' and skuName eq '{sku}' "
            f"and armRegionName eq '{region}' and priceType eq '{price_type}' "
            f"and not contains(skuName, 'Spot')"
        ),
    }
    items = requests.get(
        "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices", params=params, timeout=10
    ).json().get("Items", [])
    return items[0]["retailPrice"] if items else 0.0

def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--budget", type=float, required=True, help="Monthly budget in USD")
    parser.add_argument("--config", required=True, help="JSON file with service list")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    with open(args.config) as f:
        services = json.load(f)  # [{service, sku, region, hours, quantity}]

    total = 0.0
    for svc in services:
        price = fetch_price(svc["service"], svc["sku"], svc["region"])
        monthly = price * svc.get("hours", 730) * svc.get("quantity", 1)
        total += monthly
        print(f"  {svc['sku']:20} ${price:.4f}/hr  ${monthly:.2f}/mo")

    print(f"Estimated monthly cost: ${total:.2f} / budget: ${args.budget:.2f}")
    if total > args.budget:
        print(f"COST GATE FAILED: ${total:.2f} exceeds ${args.budget:.2f} budget")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("COST GATE PASSED")
    sys.exit(0)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

# .github/workflows/cost-gate.yml
name: Cost Gate
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  cost-check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install requests
      - name: Run cost gate
        run: python3 scripts/cost_gate.py --budget 500 --config infra/cost-config.json

VM compute is often not the largest line item. Storage (managed disks, blob, backup) and networking (egress, VPN Gateway, Load Balancer) can be significant and are commonly underestimated in calculator estimates.

import requests

def get_storage_prices(region: str = "eastus"):
    """Fetch managed disk prices for common tiers."""
    params = {
        "api-version": "2023-01-01-preview",
        "$filter": (
            f"serviceName eq 'Storage' "
            f"and armRegionName eq '{region}' "
            f"and priceType eq 'Consumption' "
            f"and (contains(skuName, 'P10') or contains(skuName, 'P20') or contains(skuName, 'P30') "
            f"or contains(skuName, 'E10') or contains(skuName, 'E20') or contains(skuName, 'E30'))"
        ),
    }
    resp = requests.get("https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices", params=params, timeout=10)
    items = resp.json().get("Items", [])
    for item in sorted(items, key=lambda x: x.get("retailPrice", 0)):
        print(f"  {item.get('skuName',''):<30} ${item.get('retailPrice',0):.4f}/{item.get('unitOfMeasure','')}")

print(f"Managed disk prices in eastus:")
get_storage_prices("eastus")

def estimate_egress_cost(gb_per_month: float, region: str = "eastus") -> float:
    """Estimate outbound data transfer (egress) cost.
    First 100 GB/month is free. Next 10 TB at ~$0.087/GB (varies by region).
    """
    params = {
        "api-version": "2023-01-01-preview",
        "$filter": (
            f"serviceName eq 'Bandwidth' "
            f"and armRegionName eq '{region}' "
            f"and priceType eq 'Consumption' "
            f"and contains(meterName, 'Outbound')"
        ),
    }
    import requests
    items = requests.get(
        "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices",
        params=params, timeout=10
    ).json().get("Items", [])

    # Find the first tier rate (>100 GB)
    rate = 0.087  # fallback default
    for item in items:
        if "Zone 1" in item.get("meterName", "") or "Global" in item.get("meterName", ""):
            rate = item.get("retailPrice", 0.087)
            break

    free_gb = 100
    billable_gb = max(0, gb_per_month - free_gb)
    cost = billable_gb * rate
    print(f"Egress: {gb_per_month:.0f} GB ({billable_gb:.0f} GB billable at ${rate:.4f}/GB) = ${cost:.2f}/mo")
    return cost

# Example: web app serving 500 GB/month outbound
estimate_egress_cost(500, "eastus")
Understanding the fields returned by the Retail Prices API helps you build accurate filters and parse the results correctly.
Always USD for the public endpoint
List price per unit (PAYG rate, no discounts)
Same as retailPrice for most items; may differ for reserved instances
ISO 8601 date when this price became active
Unique meter identifier (matches Cost Management data)
Full product name including OS and tier
Billing unit (1 Hour, 1 GB, 10 GB, etc.)

import requests

# Demonstrate all key fields for a single item
params = {
    "api-version": "2023-01-01-preview",
    "$filter": "armSkuName eq 'Standard_D4s_v5' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' and priceType eq 'Consumption' and contains(productName, 'Linux') and not contains(skuName, 'Spot')",
    "$top": 1,
}
resp = requests.get("https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices", params=params, timeout=10)
item = resp.json().get("Items", [{}])[0]

KEY_FIELDS = [
    "retailPrice", "unitOfMeasure", "armRegionName", "location",
    "meterName", "skuName", "armSkuName", "productName", "priceType",
    "effectiveStartDate", "meterId"
]
for field in KEY_FIELDS:
    print(f"  {field:<25}: {item.get(field, 'N/A')}")
The API returns at most 100 items per page. Use the NextPageLink field to iterate through all results when your query matches more than 100 items.

import requests

def fetch_all_pages(url: str, params: dict) -> list:
    """Fetch all pages from the Retail Prices API."""
    all_items = []
    while url:
        resp = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=15)
        data = resp.json()
        all_items.extend(data.get("Items", []))
        url = data.get("NextPageLink")
        params = {}  # params are already encoded in NextPageLink
    return all_items

# Fetch ALL VM prices in East US (will be multiple pages)
all_vm_prices = fetch_all_pages(
    "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices",
    {
        "api-version": "2023-01-01-preview",
        "$filter": "serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' and priceType eq 'Consumption'",
    },
)
print(f"Total VM price records in eastus: {len(all_vm_prices)}")

# Group by SKU family
from collections import Counter
families = Counter()
for item in all_vm_prices:
    sku = item.get("armSkuName", "")
    family = sku.split("_")[1][:2] if "_" in sku else "other"  # Ds, Es, Fs, etc.
    families[family] += 1

for family, count in sorted(families.items(), key=lambda x: -x[1])[:10]:
    print(f"  {family}: {count} items")
Comparing PAYG, Reservation, and AHB Programmatically

You can fetch all three pricing variants from the API in a single function and present a comparison table — useful for building internal cost dashboards or IaC tooling that recommends the optimal purchase type at deploy time.

import requests
from typing import Optional

def price_comparison(sku: str, region: str, term: str = "1 Year") -> dict:
    """
    Return PAYG, reservation, and AHB prices for a Windows Server VM SKU.
    term: '1 Year' or '3 Years'
    """
    base = "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices"
    api_v = "2023-01-01-preview"

    def query(extra_filter: str) -> Optional[float]:
        params = {
            "api-version": api_v,
            "$filter": (
                f"armSkuName eq 'Standard_{sku}' "
                f"and armRegionName eq '{region}' "
                f"and {extra_filter}"
            ),
        }
        items = requests.get(base, params=params, timeout=10).json().get("Items", [])
        return items[0]["retailPrice"] if items else None

    payg_win = query("priceType eq 'Consumption' and contains(productName, 'Windows')")
    payg_ahb = query("priceType eq 'Consumption' and contains(productName, 'Linux')")  # AHB = compute only
    ri_win = query(f"priceType eq 'Reservation' and contains(skuName, '{term}') and contains(productName, 'Windows')")
    ri_ahb = query(f"priceType eq 'Reservation' and contains(skuName, '{term}') and contains(productName, 'Linux')")

    def monthly(hr_price):
        return round(hr_price * 730, 2) if hr_price else None

    result = {
        "sku": f"Standard_{sku}",
        "region": region,
        "PAYG Windows": monthly(payg_win),
        "PAYG + AHB (Linux compute)": monthly(payg_ahb),
        f"RI {term} Windows": monthly(ri_win) if ri_win else None,
        f"RI {term} + AHB": monthly(ri_ahb) if ri_ahb else None,
    }

    # Compute savings vs PAYG Windows baseline
    baseline = result["PAYG Windows"] or 1
    for key, val in result.items():
        if isinstance(val, float) and val and key != "PAYG Windows":
            savings_pct = round((baseline - val) / baseline * 100, 1)
            print(f"  {key:<35} ${val:>8.2f}/mo  (-{savings_pct}%)")
        elif isinstance(val, float):
            print(f"  {key:<35} ${val:>8.2f}/mo  (baseline)")

    return result

print("D4s_v5 in eastus:")
price_comparison("D4s_v5", "eastus", "1 Year")

# Full break-even analysis: when does a 1-year RI pay off vs PAYG?
def ri_break_even(payg_monthly: float, ri_monthly: float, ri_upfront: float = 0) -> float:
    """
    Returns the number of months to break even on a reservation.
    payg_monthly: PAYG cost per month
    ri_monthly:   RI monthly cost (annualized / 12 for monthly billing)
    ri_upfront:   Additional upfront payment (0 for monthly RI billing)
    """
    monthly_savings = payg_monthly - ri_monthly
    if monthly_savings <= 0:
        return float("inf")  # RI never pays off
    months = (ri_upfront / monthly_savings) if ri_upfront > 0 else 0
    print(f"Monthly savings with RI: ${monthly_savings:.2f}")
    print(f"Break-even: {months:.1f} months" if ri_upfront > 0 else "Break-even: immediate (monthly billing)")
    return months

# Example: D4s_v5 Windows
# PAYG ~$140/mo, 1yr RI ~$88/mo (monthly billing)
ri_break_even(payg_monthly=140, ri_monthly=88, ri_upfront=0)

The Retail Prices API updates prices as Microsoft changes them. Cache responses for no longer than 24 hours in any cost estimation tool; prices for established SKUs rarely change more than monthly, but new SKUs and regions can shift quickly.

The Azure Pricing Calculator provides a UI for building architecture cost estimates before deployment, with support for reservation and AHB discounts. The Azure Retail Prices API gives programmatic access to the same data for scripted cost estimation, CI/CD cost gates, and multi-region price comparisons. Use the TCO Calculator when building a business case for migrating from on-premises, as it accounts for hidden infrastructure costs that a raw Azure bill comparison misses.
